description: De-palatalization of the voiced palato-alveolar affricate [dʒ] to the voiced alveolar affricate [dz]. This change involves the loss of palatal articulation, shifting the place of articulation forward. It can be seen in the adaptation of loanwords or as a historical sound change.

languageExamples.description: Adaptation of loanwords containing [dʒ], such as from Turkish or English, where it is often nativized to [dz]. For example, 'cami' (mosque) /dʒami/ becomes /dzami/.
In some historical developments within Slavic languages, the Proto-Slavic *dj evolved into [dʒ] and subsequently simplified to [dz] in certain dialects.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
